observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Inventory Committee to (i) submit the results of the inventory-
taking during the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts; and (ii) facilitate the
request for authority from the COA to derecognize the non-
existing/missing PPEs, provided that the conditions are met and supported
with complete documentation as outlined under Item 8.3 of COA Circular
No. 2020-006; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) expedite the request for the
turnover and other relevant documents of the completed infrastructure
projects implemented by DPWH – Romblon District, for the recognition in
the books of accounts; (ii) henceforth, secure the turnover and other
relevant documents within the year the infrastructure projects are
completed by the implementing agency, to reflect the true value of assets
at year-end in accordance with IPSAS 17; (iii) stop the practice of
providing residual value for the depreciable component of the road
network systems pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008; (iv) prepare the
adjusting entries for observations (c) and (d) to reflect the accurate book
value of PPE accounts as at December 31, 2024; and (v) verify the accuracy
of the calculation of depreciation expenses in the depreciation schedule.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. effect the adjusting entries to take up the reconciling items in the books of
accounts;
b. coordinate with the Acting Municipal Treasurer to recognize the two bank
accounts held in trust by the Municipality under the Trust Fund; and
c. henceforth prepare and maintain detailed SL for each bank account to
validate the correctness and accuracy of the Cash in Bank account
balances reported in the financial statements.

Given the recurring observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. issue demand letters to all AOs who are still connected with the
Municipality, directing the immediate settlement/liquidation of their
outstanding cash advances and notifying them of the withholding of
salaries in case of non-compliance thereof pursuant to COA Circular No.
97-002, and furnish the Audit Team with copies of issued demand letters
duly received by AOs;
b. strictly require the AOs to settle or liquidate the cash advances within the
prescribed period in accordance with Sections 5.1, 5.8, and 5.9 of COA
Circular No. 97-002;
c. furnish the Audit Team with copies of the lists of available records,
investigation reports, and complete documentation of the results of the
one-time cleansing of dormant accounts conducted by the Management;
and
d. request for approval of COA to write-off or derecognize from the books
the dormant accounts, provided that the procedures and supporting
documents laid down in COA Circular No. 2023-008 are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to perform the following:
a. strengthen the coordination efforts with the source agencies to (i) reconcile
the dormant unliquidated fund transfers; and (ii) ensure strict compliance
with the procedures and requirements outlined under Annex 8 of COA
Circular No. 2023-008, and request from the COA for write-off or
derecognition of dormant inter-agency fund transfers;
b. henceforth adequately prepare separate subsidiary records for each fund
transferred to the Municipality and comply with the procedures under
Sections 6.4 and 6.5 of COA Circular No. 94-013 for the regular
reconciliation of balances with source agencies; and
c. recognize the fire code fees, prizes, and fund transfers from LGUs to their
appropriate account. If the noted misstatements in observation (c) remain
outstanding to date, consider the following adjusting entry to correct the
balances of the Due to NGAs account and other affected accounts:
Account Debit Credit
Due to NGAs 744,801.58
Due to LGUs 10,000.00
Trust Liabilities 419,045.58
Government Equity 315,756.00

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. continue the practice of setting cut-off dates on the submission of
supporting documents to the Accounting Office to ensure the timely
recognition of obligations at the end of the reporting period;
b. practice the recognition of the withholding tax simultaneously with the
setting up of the obligations for goods received, services rendered, and
infrastructure projects pursuant to Section 9(c) R.A. No. 11976; and
c. evaluate the nature of obligations and classify them under the appropriate
liability accounts in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts
prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries on the above observations
to reflect the correct balances of the accounts in the financial statements, and
henceforth thoroughly analyze the accounts in the preparation of the financial
statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to provide disclosures of the reconciliation on the
actual amounts per SCBAA and SFPer on either the face of the SCBAA or
in the Notes to Financial Statements to enhance the reliability of the
financial statements; and
b. MBO and Municipal Accountant revisit the guidelines in IPSAS 24 to
ensure adequate preparation of the SCBAA, reflecting accurate and
complete changes in the budget information under the Revenue and
Expenditure sections.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct
the DO to (i) apply for an adequate amount of bond to ensure compliance with
Section 7.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and BTr Circular No. 02-2009; (ii) post
the transactions in the cashbook accurately pursuant to Section 6 of the same COA
Circular; and (iii) procure heavy duty safety vault for proper safekeeping of the
cash and cash items.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the:
a. MPDO, together with all other officials concerned, to (i) closely monitor
the implementation of development projects and ensure that the priority
projects identified in the AIP are promptly implemented to deliver
satisfactory benefits to the constituents; and (ii) coordinate with and assist
the barangays in the identification of their development projects to be
integrated in the AIP; and
b. Municipal Engineer to (i) conduct appropriate detailed engineering
activities to serve as a basis of the MPDO in adequately evaluating the
technical viability of proposed projects before inclusion in the list of
priority projects to guarantee their procurement and implementation
readiness. In this way, projects that are not feasible or have a remote
possibility of being implemented may not be considered during the budget
year; and (ii) coordinate with the MPDO to reconcile the list of completed
projects with corresponding savings to facilitate the reversion for newly
identified development projects in the Annual or Supplemental Budgets.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. MPDO and MDRRMO to ensure the proper integration of specific PPAs
to be charged to the 70% MF and STF in the AIP, in accordance with the
LDRRMFIP;
b. MDRRMO to (i) facilitate the timely implementation of PPAs under the
LDRRMF by monitoring the period of implementation, ensuring full
execution of PPAs within the year; (ii) in coordination with the Municipal
Accountant, reconcile actual disbursements reported in the RSUDRRMF
with the list of vouchers charged against the LDRRMF; and (iii) ensure
that only PPAs aligned with disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster
preparedness, disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery
are included in the LDRRMFIP; and
c. Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of transferring the unexpended
balance of CO from the current appropriations to the STF until the
projects are completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Supply Officer or end-user to require the concerned supplier to
immediately pay the amount of P230,793.82 under the observation ‘a’,
representing the supposedly imposed liquidated damages on the delayed
delivery of goods;
b. Municipal Engineer to (i) provide SWA or Inspection Report as of the
original contract expiration date to accurately determine the amount of
unperformed work to be used as a basis in computing the liquidated
damages for the delayed completion of infrastructure projects. Otherwise,
charge the contractor for the immediate settlement of the liquidated
damages totaling P787,252.58 under observation ‘b’ and ‘c’; (ii) monitor
the implementation of the infrastructure projects on a periodic basis and
conduct an inspection on the date of original contract expiration to
determine the slippage and the amount of unperformed works; and (iii)
submit the necessary supporting documents related to the grant of contract
time extensions under the observation ‘d’ to validate the approval of the
requests and determine whether the contractors may be relieved from
paying the liquidated damages amounting to P323,414.00;
c. Municipal Accountant to henceforth deduct liquidated damages from any
money due to the suppliers and contractors. In case full settlements have
been made without deducting liquidated damages, issue a demand letter to
the suppliers/contractors for the payment thereof. Failure to exercise any
of the appropriate actions will cause the issuance of Notice of Charge on
the noted deficiencies; and
d. Municipal Engineer to (i) conduct adequate detailed engineering and other
preventive measures to resolve the existing issues that are currently
delaying the completion of projects, instead of granting the supplier and
contractor contract time extension without proper evaluation and
documentation; (ii) ensure that the approval of contract time extension are
documented separately, indicating whether the liquidated damages would
be imposed for the delayed delivery of goods and completion of
infrastructure projects; (iii) see to it that the grant of contract time
extensions are completely supported with documents, including the basis
for the number of days allowed for the extension; and (iv) alternatively,
consider rescinding or terminating a contract and forfeit contractor’s
performance security for negative slippage more than 15 percent resulting
from contractor’s fault or negligence at the time of inspection.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the:
a. BAC Chairperson to (i) adopt public bidding in the procurement of bulk
POL items and Direct Retail Purchase for non-bulk POL items; (ii)
explore feasible options to enhance public access and transparency in
public bidding, such as live broadcasting of the proceedings via social
media or other platforms; and (iii) address the deficiencies observed in the
procurement process to ensure competitiveness, transparency, and
integrity for promoting cost-efficiency without compromising project
standards;
b. Municipal Engineer to (i) rigorously review the documentary requirements
submitted by the contractors to ensure complete, accurate, and consistent
information; (ii) conduct proper inspections for the procurement involving
goods with installation services and prepare the corresponding Inspection
Report the same extent as that of infrastructure projects; (iii) ensure that
inspection is immediately made on the contract expiry date; and (iv)
conduct inspections and prepare Inspection Reports for the payment of
labor components of the projects implemented by administration;
c. MDRRMO to cease the practice of claiming reimbursement for the snacks
and meals consumed during DRRM activities. Instead, properly plan the
activities and issue a Purchase Request to enable the procurement of the
goods through SVP or other alternative modes of procurement, wherever
appropriate;
d. MBO to diligently indicate the correct obligation number in the CAFOA
that corresponds to the source of funds (100-General Fund and 200-Special
Education Fund) to prevent any issues in the reconciliation of actual
amounts between the budget and accounting records; and
e. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the lacking documents noted in
observation ‘d’ and ensure that all supporting documents are complete
prior to processing any payments to the contractors. In case of deficiencies,
immediately notify the concerned contractor for clarification or
submission of the revised document; (ii) demand the contractor to
immediately recover the advanced payment amounting to P419,807.10.
Otherwise, enforce the surety bond with the bonding company and pursue
appropriate action against the defaulting contractor; and (iii) require the
suppliers to post warranty security for the procurement of goods.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to (i) analyze and reconcile the balances of the Due
to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to PhilHealth accounts;
(ii) establish effective control systems in the recording and monitoring of
these accounts to ensure that entries made thereon are accurate, complete,
and reconciled with the source documents and facilitate timely detection of
errors; and (iii) facilitate the immediate settlement of the overdue
unremitted balances to the concerned government agencies after effecting
the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts; and
b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the full
amount of taxes withheld and mandatory contributions deducted are
remitted intact to the concerned government agencies within the
prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) require the Municipal
Engineer to ensure that all infrastructure projects are provided with the project
signboards that adhere to the prescribed format with complete information,
including periodic updates on project status, to achieve the primary objectives of
posting project signboards; and (ii) inform the Audit Team within 10 days after
the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the project that the
appropriate project signboards or public notices are already posted for validation
and inspection purposes. Alternatively, the BAC may attach to the contract and
supporting documents a picture of the project signboard posted at the project site.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following officers to
perform their respective duties and functions (a) BAC to (i) guarantee that all
procurement are aligned with the current year’s APP and conducted in accordance
with the planned schedule; (ii) customize every content, samples, and instructions
provided by the PBDs to reflect the requirements of the procurement at hand in
the preparation of bidding documents; (iii) refrain from brand referencing in the
procurement of goods; (iv) conduct a Pre-procurement Conference for PPAs with
ABC exceeding P2,000,000.00 for goods and services to address the issues noted in
the procurement planning; (v) ensure that the Pre-bid Conference is conducted
seven calendar days after the posting of Invitation to Bid and 12 calendar days
before the deadline for bid submission; (vi) ensure that NOA and NTP are
promptly posted in the PhilGEPS website within three and 15 calendar days,
respectively, after their issuances; (vii) ensure that the performance security and
documentary requirements are provided by the winning bidder before the
Municipality enters into a contract; and (viii) furnish the Audit Team with a copy
of contracts and POs, including supporting documents, within five working days
from the execution/issuance thereof; (b) BAC TWG to intensify the conduct of
post-qualification to validate the veracity of the legal, technical, and financial
requirements submitted by the bidder. The PER must be supported with any form
of documentation deemed acceptable to demonstrate the actions taken during
post-qualification activity; and (c) Municipal Engineer to integrate appropriate
documentation for the detailed engineering activities as required under Annex “A”
of the Revised IRR in the procurement of infrastructure projects as the basis for
the completion of adequately customized bidding documents by the BAC.

instruct the BAC to (i) ensure that the APP reflects the necessary information on the entire procurement activities that it plans to undertake within the calendar year as provided under DBM Circular No. 2010-9 dated December 30, 2010; (ii) observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 specifically in the procurement of Consulting Services and conform with the standard forms and manuals prescribed by the GPPB to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; (iii) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five working days from the execution thereof as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; and (iv) submit the lacking documents noted above; and

instruct the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of requirements before processing any claims relative to the project to ascertain validity and legality of the transaction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to carefully analyze and review the accounting entries made on the accounts affected, reconcile the reciprocal accounts and effect appropriate adjustments thereof to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data on the agency’s financial position.

observations.recommendation:
a. direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the complete physical count of
all the inventories and property, plant and equipment of the Municipality
and prepare the corresponding inventory reports which should include the
unserviceable properties. The committee shall reconcile the results of the
count with the property records and accounting records and submit the
complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the auditor concerned,
pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. require the Municipal Accountant to determine the estimated useful life of
School Buildings and Sports Equipment accounts and provide the
corresponding allowance for depreciation;
c. require the Property Custodian and Accounting Unit to maintain property
cards and PPE ledger cards, respectively, indicating the details quantities,
exact date of acquisition, and status/condition of all PPEs and conduct
periodic reconciliation to bring both records into agreement;
For inventory items, maintenance of the supplies ledger cards by the
Municipal Accountant and stock cards by the Treasurer or General
37
Services Officer should be required to ensure a check and balance in the
issuance of supplies and materials; and
d. advise the Inventory Committee to (i) report all the unserviceable
properties in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable
Property (IIRUP) supported with proper documentation for subsequent
disposal of same in accordance with COA rules and regulations; and (ii)
strictly comply with the guidelines/procedures set forth under COA
Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the purpose of cleansing
the enormous amount of discrepancies in the PPE account balances of the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to cancel the unclaimed stale checks and furnish the Municipal
Accountant with the original DVs and its supporting documents for the
preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) following the procedures
prescribed above to ensure recording of such cancellation. For checks which
became stale in the hands of the payee for many years, consider the reversion of
these payables to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. thoroughly check the entries made before signing the Journal Entry
Vouchers (JEVs) to avoid errors in recording. Henceforth, observe proper
accounting for Real Property/Special Education Taxes;
b. make efforts in tracing the accounting errors made in previous years
and effect the necessary adjustments; and
c. guide the personnel-in-charge of the preparation of ledgers of individual
accounts to ensure correctness of the presentation of SET accounts in the
financial statements in compliance with Section 111 of P.D. No. 1445 and
Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

a. for cash advances of accountable officers who are still connected with the
agency, require immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances.
Failure on the part of accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate
their cash advances shall constitute a valid ground for the withholding of
the payment of any money due them;
b. for unsettled accounts of personnel who are deceased, have already
retired or no longer in service, have transferred to other agencies and
those whose status or whereabouts could not be determined, exhaust all
remedies to collect by sending confirmation, notification or demand
letters to settle the unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, apply for write
off in accordance with the guidelines and procedures prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 29, 2016; and
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify long outstanding
cash advances in the financial statements in accordance with paragraphs
70 and 76 of IPSAS I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. expedite the process of ascertaining the collectability of the long-
outstanding and dormant accounts; and
b. evaluate and identify long-outstanding and dormant accounts which are
eligible for write-off and comply with the procedures provided in COA
Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

a. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare breakdown or details of Due to
NGAs account and make necessary adjustment for errors identified,
unreconciled amount and unaccounted balances, if any; (ii) maintain a
subsidiary ledger for each fund transfer received to readily determine
information on each fund and easily track its transactions and movements
including related cash and payables; and (iii) review the regularity and
completeness of supporting documents of disbursement vouchers before
processing claims for payment; and
b. Municipal Engineer to submit the above-enumerated lacking documents
for the mentioned infrastructure projects. Henceforth, ensure the
completeness and accuracy of supporting documents prior to processing
of payments.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the members of the BAC and its Secretariat to:
a. henceforth, adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 on the general policy on competitive bidding to promote effective
competition and transparency in the procurement process and obtain a
fair and equitable contract for the interest of the government;
b. deliberate properly on the appropriate mode of procurement to be used
and resort only to alternative modes of procurement if the conditions
under Sections 48-54, Rule XVI of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 are met;
c. inform all concerned departments to include only those PPAs subject of
procurement in the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP); and
d. indicate the time schedule for each identified procurement activity for
public bidding for all projects to efficiently manage the conduct of the
procurement transactions.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our previous
recommendations that:
a. The Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC) together with all other employees concerned to (i)
closely monitor the implementation of development projects and see to it
that the priority projects identified in the Annual Investment Plan are
promptly implemented in order to deliver satisfactory benefits to the
constituents; and (ii) ensure that the reports on the status of government
PPAs submitted to the Audit Team quarterly includes all on-going and
planned PPAs for the year and that the information provided therein are
accurate to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation thereof;
b. The Municipal Engineer conduct appropriate detailed engineering
activities to serve as basis of the MPDC in properly evaluating the
technical viability of proposed projects before inclusion in the list of
priority projects to be included in the 20 percent DF. In this way, projects
with issues, not feasible or has a remote possibility of being implemented
may not be considered during the budget year;
c. The BAC ensure that all PPAs to be subjected for procurement are
included in the APP pursuant to Section 7.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of
the R.A. No. 9184; and
d. The Municipal Accountant be guided by the requirements of Section 4(6)
of P.D. No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 474 b(5) of
R.A. No. 7160 for proper documentation of claims against government
funds and submit the lacking documents mentioned above to establish the
propriety of the disbursements.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the GAD Focal Person to (i) ensure that total allocation for GAD
PPAs to be implemented during the year shall conform with the actual
amount provided for GAD in the approved budget; (ii) comply with
PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2020-05 dated September 11, 2020 in
the attribution of the GAD Budget; (iii) coordinate with the Accounting
Office in the preparation of the GAD AR in order to come up with
accurate and reliable reports; (iv) undertake proper planning in the
procurement of goods and supplies for the implementation of the GAD
PPAs; (v) ensure that rental of house which served as Bahay Kanlungan
undergo proper procurement procedures as required under Item 9, Annex
H of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; (vi) refrain from procuring goods
or supplies on reimbursement basis; and (vii) henceforth, strictly observe
the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to promote
transparency, economy and efficiency in the procurement process;
b. through its GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), exhaust all available
resources for the collection and processing of data requirements of the
GAD Database;
c. also, through the GFPS and in coordination with the Municipal Planning
and Development Office (MPDO), consider the allocation of funds for the
construction of building that could be used as Bahay Kanlungan as this
could prove more economical for the Municipality in the long run; and
d. the Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the GAD Focal Person in
the preparation of the GAD AR; (ii) check the completeness and veracity
of supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
Section 474(b)(5) of P.D. No. 1445; and (iii) be guided with COA Circular
No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 which provides for the Revised
Chart of Accounts for the LGUs in the recording of transactions.

a. the BAC to: (i) submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team for
evaluation; (ii) check the latest issuances of the GPPB relative to the
PBDs and other standard forms and ensure that bidding documents
issued to prospective bidders are up-to-date; (iii) strengthen internal
control over the evaluation of bids and post-qualification of bidders; (iv)
henceforth adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of
R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of the procurement process and ensure that all
bid documents are complete, evaluated, and submitted on time; and (v)
furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract Agreement and its
attachments within five working days from execution thereof as required
under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
b. the personnel assigned in the preparation of the POW and the Municipal
Engineer to (i) properly fill-out and review all information/details
required in order to facilitate technical review of the project; and (ii)
comply strictly with DPWH DO No. 197 s. 2016 in the preparation of the
Approved Budget for the Contract.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the MSWM Focal
Person:
a. fast track the preparation of the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan
(SCRP) for the existing final disposal facility as required for the approval
of the Municipalitys 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan;
b. strictly enforce existing laws and ordinances on proper solid waste
management in order to achieve community compliance. Impose
appropriate fines and penalties, where necessary;
c. ensure that MRFs in all barangays are properly managed; and
d. provide collectors and other personnel directly dealing with the collection
of solid wastes with personal protective equipment to safeguard them from
health hazards.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and
Municipal Budget Officer to ensure the inclusion of specific COVID-19
related PPAs in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget,
respectively, of the Municipality;
b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit all lacking bid documents
to the Audit Team for evaluation to prove the regularity and legality of the
procurement process; and
c. Municipal Accountant to (i) check the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims in compliance with
Section 474(b)(5) of P.D. No. 1445; (ii) coordinate with the personnel in
charge of the implementation of COVID-19 PPAs and submit the lacking
documents noted above; and (iii) henceforth, use the "Other MOOE"
account only for expenditures that cannot be classified under specific
MOOE accounts and observe the use of proper accounts in the recording
of the financial transactions of the LGU guided with COA Circular No.
2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 which provides for the Revised Chart
of Accounts for the LGUs in the recording of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the persons liable to settle immediately the audit disallowances
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. For
those who have already retired or were separated from service after the
issuance of the Notices of Finality of Decision (NFD), strictly require an
Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities prior to
processing of their claims for terminal pay in order that their
accountabilities could be appropriately settled. For those who are no
longer connected or working with the local government unit prior to the
issuance of the NFD, direct the Municipal Accountant/Municipal
Treasurer to combine efforts in locating them and in sending
communications demanding full settlement of their accountabilities.
The Municipality is apprised of Section 16.3 of the 2009 RRSA
which provides that the liability of persons determined to be liable under
an ND shall be solidary and the Commission may go against any person
liable without prejudice to the latters claim against the rest of the
persons liable. Underscoring ours;
b. for installment payment as in the case of partial settlement made
through payroll deduction, ensure that request for authority from COA
to settle the disallowances in installment basis should be made by the
persons liable, the head of agency or its authorized official pursuant to
COA Resolution No. 2015-031 as amended by COA Resolution No. 2017-
021; and
c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to support recorded disallowances
with subsidiary ledgers/records containing proper disclosure on the
reference NFD, persons liable, and amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the complete physical count of all the inventories and property, plant and equipment of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding inventory reports which should include the unserviceable properties.  The committee shall reconcile the results of the count with the property records and accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the auditor concerned, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;

Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and submit a separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for DRRM QRF, MOOE and CO in compliance with Item 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-02.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:

require the persons liable to settle immediately the audit disallowances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006.  For those who have already retired or were separated from service after the issuance of the Notices of Finality of Decision (NFD), strictly require an Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities prior to processing of their claims for terminal pay in order that their accountabilities could be appropriately settled.  For those who are no longer connected or working with the local government unit prior to the issuance of the NFD, direct the Municipal Accountant/Municipal Treasurer to combine efforts in locating them and in sending communications demanding full settlement of their accountabilities.
The Municipality is apprised of Section 16.3 of the 2009 RRSA which provides that the liability of persons determined to be liable under an ND shall be solidary and the Commission may go against any person liable without prejudice to the latter’s claim against the rest of the persons liable. Underscoring ours;
for installment payment as in the case of partial settlement made through payroll deduction, ensure that request for authority from COA to settle the disallowances in installment basis should be made by the persons liable, the head of agency or its authorized official pursuant to COA Resolution No. 2015-031 as amended by COA Resolution No. 2017-021; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to support recorded disallowances with subsidiary ledgers/records containing proper disclosure on the reference NFD, persons liable, and amount.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:

a.	direct the inventory committee to conduct the complete physical count of all the inventories and property, plant and equipment of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding inventory reports which may include unserviceable properties.  The committee shall reconcile the results of the count with the property records and accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCI and RPCPPE to the auditor concerned, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;

b.	require the Property Custodian and Accounting Unit to maintain property cards and PPE ledger cards, respectively, indicating the details quantities, exact date of acquisition, and status/condition of all PPEs and conduct periodic reconciliation to bring both records into agreement.

For inventory items, maintenance of the supplies ledger cards by the Municipal Accountant and stock cards by the Treasurer or General Services Officer should be required to ensure a check and balance in the issuance of supplies and materials; and

c.	advise the Inventory Committee to report all the unserviceable properties in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) supported with proper documentation for subsequent disposal of same in accordance with COA rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction:
A Municipal Inventory Committee was created and said Committee have already planned and discussed the activities based on procedural guidelines to execute the inventory taking efficiently and accurately.  Instructions were also given to the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventory items and monitor and report the distribution and consumption of items.
The Office of the Municipal Accountant and Supply Office were also instructed to reconcile their records and come up with a reconciled report.
observations.reason:
The Municipality still failed to conduct complete physical count of all the inventories and property, plant and equipment.

The result of the physical count of PPE was still not reconciled with the accounting records.

Unserviceable properties were not yet disposed.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing audit observations, we reiterated our recommendation and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a.	require all accountable officers and employees to immediately settle their outstanding cash advances; instruct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the overdue accounts and render report on the status of the demand letters sent to enforce collection thereof to protect the interest of the government. Section IV. General Guidelines of COA Memorandum No. 2017-010 dated May 15, 2017 provides that: xxx. The Audit Team Leader (ATL) shall request from the Accountant proof of exhaustion of all remedies to collect receivables and liquidate cash advances and fund transfers as basis for the issuance of the final demand letter.  There is therefore a need for the Accountant to issue demand letter to the AO/employee/recipient/NGOs to liquidate their due and demandable cash advances/fund transfers;

b.	direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee unless previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and  

c.	require the officials and employees to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period provided in Section 5 of COA Circular 97-002.
observations.managementAction:
The Local Government of Odiongan has already made a series of issuance of demand letters pertaining to unliquidated cash advances of various employees in CY 2020 which resulted to settlement and payment of P17,074.00. Unfortunately last March 16-May 31, 2020 the plans and programs to collect and settle unliquidated cash advances were hampered due to the declaration of Enhanced Community Quarantine in view of COVID-19 pandemic. However, when the Inter-Agency task Force issued an MGCQ status for the Province of Romblon, the Municipality pursued the collection process and issuance of demand letters to accountable officers.

The Municipal Mayor issued Office Order dated March 22, 2018 to all municipal officials and employees re: Liquidation of cash advances.
observations.reason: Cash advances totaling P2,010,681.21 remained unliquidated as at December 31, 2020.

 d. Provide collectors  and  other  personnel  directly  dealing  with  the
    collection of  solid  wastes  with  personal  protective  equipment  to
    safeguard them from health hazards; and

 e. Fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation  of
    a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of  R.A.  No.
    9003  the  soonest  possible  time  to  avert   adverse   environmental
    consequences.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended and  the
  management agreed that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

     a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to
        comply with the provisions COA Circular 2012-002 in the  preparation
        of LDRRMFIP and R.A. No. 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint  Memorandum
        Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 for proper  utilization  of
        the fund.

            Proper classification of PPAs in the LDRRMFIP  should  also  be
        given careful attention such that PPAs which are capital  in  nature
        and are intended to be implemented in the ensuing years be  properly
        classified as Capital Outlay; and

     b. Municipal Accountant to (i) comply with the accounting and reporting
        guidelines for LDRRM Fund set forth under COA Circular No.  2012-002
        dated September 12, 2012; (ii)  account  inventory  items  to  their
        corresponding  inventory  accounts  and  record  the   corresponding
        expense  only  upon  issuance/consumption  thereof;  (iii)   observe
        review the regularity and completeness of  supporting  documents  of
        disbursement vouchers prescribed under Section 9.1.3 of COA Circular
        No. 2012 - 001 before processing claims for payment; (iv) submit the
        aforementioned lacking documents to the  Audit  Team  to  prove  the
        transaction’s validity, accuracy, regularity and propriety; and  (v)
        submit the notes comprising  a  summary  of  significant  accounting
        policies and other explanatory notes.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:


      a.  instruct the concerned municipal officials/personnel to fast track
         the implementation of the E-trike project so that  expected  output
         from the project could be realized;


      b.  require the Municipal Accountant  to  record  the  amount  of  the
         donated E-trike units in the books of the Municipality  to  reflect
         the correct balances of the  affected  accounts  in the  financial
         statements; and


      c.  prepare immediately the  required  Property  Transfer  Report  and
         submit the same, together with the Journal Entry Voucher  (JEV)  to
         the donor agency for them to facilitate proper  accounting  of  the
         donated e-trike units in their books.

We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:


      a. require  the  persons  liable  to  settle  immediately  the  audit
         disallowances pursuant to COA Circular No.  2009-006.   For  those
         who have already retired or were separated from service after  the
         issuance of the Notices of Finality of  Decision  (NFD),  strictly
         require   an   Office   Clearance   from   Money   and    Property
         accountabilities prior to processing of their claims for  terminal
         pay in order that their accountabilities  could  be  appropriately
         settled.  For those who are no longer connected  or  working  with
         the local government unit prior to the issuance of the NFD, direct
         the Municipal Accountant/Municipal Treasurer to combine efforts in
         locating  them  and  in  sending  communications  demanding   full
         settlement of their accountabilities.

                The Municipality is apprised of Section  16.3  of  the  2009
         RRSA which provides that the liability of persons determined to be
         liable under an ND shall be solidary and  the  Commission  may  go
         against any person liable without prejudice to the latter’s  claim
         against the rest of the persons liable. Underscoring ours;

      b. for installment payment as in the case of partial settlement  made
         through payroll deduction, ensure that request for authority  from
         COA to settle the disallowances in  installment  basis  should  be
         made by the persons liable, the head of agency or  its  authorized
         official pursuant to COA Resolution No. 2015-031 as amended by COA
         Resolution No. 2017-021; and


      c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to support recorded disallowances
         with subsidiary ledgers/records containing proper disclosure on the
         reference NFD, persons liable, and amount.
Financial and Compliance Audit

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through its Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to show the value of the assets acquired charged against the transferred funds from NGAs to present the correct financial condition of the Property, Plant and Equipment account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe the proper accounting for transfer of PPE accounts. Prepare the necessary adjustment to correct the balances of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position of General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the computation of the depreciation expense with a lapsing schedule so as to ensure the accuracy of the amount provided and to monitor the correct balance of PPE accounts.

comply with the recommendation.

Require all accountable officers and employees to settle/liquidate all their outstanding cash advances by sending confirmation, notification or demand letters; c. In case of failure to settle/liquidate their cash advances, cause or order the withholding of the payment of their salaries as provided for under Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and impose the sanctions provided under the penal provision of Section 128 of PD No. 1445; and d. Require an Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities from all personnel who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death benefits.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through its
Inventory Committee, conduct complete physical count of the
properties of the Municipality, reconcile the results of the count
with the accounting records and submit the complete and accurate
RPCPPE to the Audit Team, pursuant to Section 124 of the
Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare
the necessary adjusting journal entries to show the value of the
assets acquired charged against the transferred funds from NGAs
to present the correct financial condition of the Property, Plant
and Equipment account.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe
the proper accounting for transfer of PPE accounts. Prepare the
necessary adjustment to correct the balances of the affected
accounts in the Statement of Financial Position of General Fund.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant support
the computation of the depreciation expense with a lapsing
schedule so as to ensure the accuracy of the amount provided and
to monitor the correct balance of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the
submission of all paid disbursement vouchers to the Auditor’s Office in
compliance with the prescribed mode of submission pursuant to Section 6.05
of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor impose all available actions as provided by COA Circular No. 2012-
004 on the settlement of cash advances.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. intensify the monitoring of cash advances;
b. see to it existing provisions relative to the granting and liquidation
of cash advances are strictly complied with; and
c. stop the grant of additional cash advances to any official or
employee with outstanding unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace the erroneous
entries made for CY 2018 which led to the abnormal account balances and
effect the necessary adjusting entries to ensure fairness in the presentation of
the Financial Statements. Likewise, observe the proper accounting for Real
Property Taxes pursuant to Section 19 of the NGAs Manual for LGUs,
Volume 1 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the ensuing years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records with
GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PHILHEALTH and make the necessary
adjustments, if any;
b. require the employee concerned to observe the remittance of
mandatory contributions within the prescribed period to avoid
payment of penalties; and
c. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to identify the employees whose
GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PHILHEALTH contributions or loan
amortizations were still not remitted and effect the immediate
remittance thereof.

We recommended that the Management submit the documentary
requirements to support the payments for the purchased lot as prescribed
under COA Circular 2012-001 and other pertinent provisions. Failure to
submit the same would constitute a valid ground for the issuance of Notice of
Suspensions for the subject transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
concerned LGU personnel to carefully and judiciously plan all the
projects to be implemented to avoid wastage of government funds and
to deliver satisfactory service to the public. Adhere strictly to the
Implementing Guidelines on the Lease of Privately-owned Real
property prior to entering into Lease Contract.
We also recommended that the Management strictly comply
with the provisions of COA-Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12,
2009 requiring submission of copy of contract and each of all the
documents forming part thereof to the Office of the Auditor within five
working days from the execution thereof.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant
observe the proper use of account in recording transactions. Prepare
the necessary adjusting entry to correct the data in the agency’s
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require
the officials and employees to attach the official receipts instead of
RER or Certification of Expenses not Requiring Receipts for
claims of taxi fares to ensure that the amount claimed is accurate
and proper.
We recommended that the Management exercise prudence
in granting reimbursements for expenses for out of town travels to
ensure that travel claims are within the allowed limit pursuant to
Executive Order No. 248 as amended. Claims for reimbursement
of actual travel expenses in excess of the travel expenses
authorized in the cited regulation shall be supported by a
certification of the head of agency concerned as to absolute
necessity of the claim in the performance of an assignment, and
with valid bills and receipts.
We recommended that the Municipal Vice-Mayor and the
Sangguniang Bayan Members exercise prudence in spending
government funds specifically in the conduct of conventions,
trainings, seminars and similar activities where participants come
from the same province. As much as possible, conduct provincial
meetings/seminars/conventions within the province or utilize
available government facilities, to minimize expenses.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant
scrutinize the official receipts and other documents supporting the
claims for travelling expenses prior to processing for payment to
avoid excessive payment thereof.

We recommended and the management agreed to strictly observe the
provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 in all government
procurement.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee submit
written explanation relative to the deficiencies noted above.
We also recommended that the BAC strictly adhere to the rules and
regulations set forth in the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that the BAC adopt the latest standard
bidding documents prescribed by the GPPB in compliance with Section
17.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;
We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee comply
strictly to the provisions of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of
the Republic Act No. 9184 in all stages of procurement.
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
submit to the Audit Team the above-listed lacking documents.
We recommended that:
a. the Municipal Accountant adhere with the accounting policy
of recording in the Trust Fund Books funds transferred to
the Municipality; and
b. the Municipal Mayor direct the officers concerned to fast
tract the implementation of the projects, programs and
activities funded by the National Government Agencies and
cause the immediate liquidation of the funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere
to the provisions of DBM BC 2007-003 in the payment of honoraria to
BAC and TWG to avoid disallowances in audit.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the
aforementioned documents to support the payment of BAC honoraria.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure
that subsequent payments of honoraria are supported with the
necessary documents required under Section 5.7.1 of COA Circular
2012-001.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) facilitate
establishment and maintenance of GAD database to have an effective basis
for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and to
undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data for proper
identification of existing gender issues to ensure that the approved PPAs to
be incorporated in the GPB are responsive to address the same.

We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that the
Municipal Government refrain from charging against the LDRRMF
projects and activities that are not included or related to the DRRM
activities as declared under Section 5.0 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC
No. 2013-1 and other relevant provisions.
We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the
Municipal Accountant adhere strictly to the accounting and reporting
guidelines for LDRRM Fund set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-
002 dated September 12, 2012 so as to present reliable data in the
LGU’s financial statements.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee:
a) submit written explanation relative to the deficiencies noted above
for evaluation of the Audit Team as to the next audit action;
b) submit the lacking documents noted above;
c) strictly adhere to the provisions of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184
and other relevant rules and regulations governing the
procurement of infrastructure projects.
We also recommended that the TWG assess the sufficiency of the
manpower and equipment capacity of the bidders prior to awarding the
contracts. Disqualify all future bids whose key personnel and equipment
were already pledged/used in other on-going projects or projects to be
awarded to avoid unnecessary delays in project completion and to ensure
quality project outcome.
Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the
supporting documents attached to the Disbursement Vouchers and ensure
their completeness and validity prior to processing for payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: observe the timely submission of the Financial Statements and its supporting schedules to the Audit Team. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor look into the possible cause of the delayed preparation and submission of the said reports and take immediate action to solve this problem.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was able to submit the CY 2018 financial reports and its supporting schedules before the deadline set to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: a. give priority to the timely submission of the accounts within the first ten (10) days of the ensuing month as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 and COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the management of the deficiencies noted, if any; b. submit the unsubmitted disbursement vouchers. Likewise, ensure the completeness of the DVs including their supporting documents before submitting the same to the Office of the Auditor in order to facilitate the audit of the transactions and enable us to ascertain the legality, validity and propriety of the vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor impose all available actions as provided by COA Circular No. 2012-004 on the settlement of cash advances. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant intensify the monitoring of cash advances and see to it that existing provisions relative to the granting and liquidation of cash advances are strictly complied with. Stop the grant of additional cash advances to any official or employee with outstanding unliquidated cash advances. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of granting cash advance to elective officials other than their travelling expenses pursuant to Section 4.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the fundamental principles governing the disbursements of public fund. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. require the drivers of the motor vehicles to properly accomplish the daily trip tickets for each motor vehicle and ensure that the same is approved and signed by the end-user/passengers of the motor vehicle, be serially numbered and summarized at the end of the month in the Monthly Report of Official Travel and submitted to COA for audit purposes; and b. require the person in charge of the preparation of Fuel Consumption Report to present the relevant/necessary data stated in the above cited guidelines. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure strict adherence to the provision of COA Circular No. 77-61 for the proper accounting and control of fuel consumption of government vehicles.

Request for authority of COA for write off of such undocumented long outstanding receivables through a Sangguniang Resolution, in line with COA Resolution No. 2003-002, dated January 30, 2003, stating the reasons for write off and extent of follow-up made. observations.managementAction: The Management conducted physical count of inventory for CY 2016, however, they failed to furnish the Audit Team a copy of the report up to this writing. Transfer of fund from Trust Fund to General Fund of DRR Equipment was effected by Accounting Department.
observations.reason:
The staff from Supply Office commented that they were not able to furnish a copy of the Report on time due
some revisions made by them.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise his power to take legal actions against erring officials and employees; issue demand letter to the retired Municipal treasurer to settle her cash accountability based on accounting records; otherwise, initiate the filing of appropriate charges to recover the funds of the Municipal Government as mandated in the afore-cited provisions of R.A. No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Partial settlement was made by the Assistant Municipal Treasurer through RATA deductions every month.
observations.reason:
No reason was given by management on the unsettled cash
 accountability of the deceased former Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:



1. Furnish to the Audit Team a copy of MOA/Trust Agreement and other pertinent documents that would validate the transfer, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.


2. Observe the limitations in the transfer of funds. Refrain from paying honoraria and other incentives out of the transferred funds, expressly prohibited under COA Circular No. 94-013.


3. Adhere strictly to the guidelines on the transfer, implementation and liquidation of fund transfers, set forth under COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality furnished the Audit Team copies of MOA and refrained from paying honoraria out of transferred fund for CY 2016. However, they only returned P273,000.00 out of P8,897,882.10 unused/unexpended balance of the fund to the source agency.
observations.reason: No reason was given by management for not implementing all audit recommendations

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to maintain a special account in the General Fund for the operation of the Municipality’s economic enterprises through the use of complete set of subsidiary ledger in accordance with Section 313 of RA 7160 and Sections 106 and 107 of the NGAS Manual, Volume 1 to facilitate the audit and verification of the transactions incurred by the said economic enterprises and to enable the Management to readily determine and evaluate its results of operation and financial condition.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality furnished the Audit Team a copy of SB Resolution No. 2016-83 dated November 15, 2016 enacting an ordinance creating the Municipal Economic Enterprise Development Office of Odiongan, Romblon through Ordinance No. 2016-16. Moreover, the Accounting Office informed the Audit Team that they implement the audit recommendation this CY 2017.
